title: He Kōrero Tuku Iho: The Living Stories of Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku / Indigenous Narratives: The Living Stories of the Mangakōtukutuku Stream
subject: English
culturalContext: Students will explore the pūrākau (traditional stories) and mātauranga Māori associated with Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku, the stream that flows through our rohe and gives our college its name. This awa is a vital taonga to Ngāti Kahungunu, with deep connections to local history, navigation, traditional food gathering practices, and environmental stewardship.
learningObjectives: Analyze and compare traditional oral narratives about Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku with contemporary written accounts, identifying key themes and cultural perspectives.
Create an original piece of creative writing (narrative or poetry) that incorporates elements of local pūrākau and demonstrates understanding of place-based storytelling.
Collaborate effectively in a small group to plan, conduct, and present findings from an interview with a local knowledge holder about the awa.
Use descriptive language and sensory details to vividly capture the significance of a local natural feature in written form.

activities.title: He Whakarongo: Listening to the Awa
activities.description: Students visit Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku (or a designated viewing area). In silence, they use a 'senses map' template to record what they see, hear, smell, and feel. Back in class, they share observations and brainstorm descriptive vocabulary (kupu) in both English and Te Reo Māori to capture the essence of the place.

activities.title: He Pānui Pūrākau: Unpacking the Stories
activities.description: In small groups, students read and analyze different versions of the pūrākau of Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku (provided texts). Using a graphic organizer, they identify key characters, events, settings, and the underlying values or lessons (tikanga) each story conveys. Groups then jigsaw to share their findings with the class.

activities.title: He Uiui: Interviewing a Kaitiaki
activities.description: A local kaumātua or kaitiaki (e.g., from the local marae or environmental group) visits the class (in-person or via video call) to share their knowledge and personal connections to the awa. Students prepare thoughtful questions beforehand, practice active listening during the interview, and take notes for their final project.

activities.title: He Tuhi: Our Place, Our Story
activities.description: Students plan and draft an original piece of writing (e.g., a short story, a poem, a diary entry from the perspective of someone from the past) inspired by the pūrākau, their visit to the awa, and the interview. They are encouraged to weave in descriptive language, cultural elements, and a personal connection to the place.

activities.title: He Whakaaturanga: Sharing Our Mahi
activities.description: Students present their final written pieces to their peers in a small 'author's circle', providing and receiving constructive feedback. Selected pieces may be compiled into a class book or displayed alongside a map of the awa in the school library or wharenui.

resources: Transcripts and recordings of local pūrākau for Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku, sourced from the Ngāti Kahungunu iwi website or local library archives.
A scheduled session with a kaumātua or representative from a local marae (e.g., Omahu Marae) or environmental group like Ngāti Kahungunu Ki Heretaunga Tāmui Tāui.
"Waiata and Pūrākau of Hawke's Bay" by local authors or iwi publications.
Digital voice recorders or tablets for conducting and recording interviews and observations.

tasks: A completed interview notes template and reflective paragraph on the process.
A final, polished piece of original creative writing based on the awa and its stories.
A brief self-assessment on their collaboration and contribution to group tasks.

nzcAlignment: English: Listening, Reading, and Viewing - Processes and strategies; Ideas; Language features.
English: Speaking, Writing, and Presenting - Processes and strategies; Ideas; Language features; Structure.
The Arts: Understanding the Arts in Context - Explore and describe how cultures and stories are recorded and passed on through oral, written, and visual forms.
Social Sciences: Understand how people remember and record the past in different ways.
